sources.label: Vermont Genealogy — Biography of Rev. James Hobart

residence.note: Settled May 1789 from Hebron CT; farmed 1300 acres at Scott Hill/East Road intersection in East Road School District.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Established the 1300-acre East Road farmstead and built permanent buildings here after settling Berlin VT in 1789.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT.

narrative_blocks.header: Settled Berlin Vermont with collapsed-roof cabin
narrative_blocks.body: Zachariah served in the Revolutionary War with the 8th company of the 12th Connecticut militia, then in February-March 1789 led his family from Hebron Connecticut to Berlin Vermont — up the Connecticut River with two teams of oxen, switching from wheels to runners in Brookfield. They found the log cabin he had built the previous summer crushed under three or four feet of snow.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded a 1300-acre farm still in family
narrative_blocks.body: Zachariah's 1300 acres in the East Road School District of Berlin VT — at what became the southeast corner of East Road and Scott Hill Road — passed down through generations of Perrins. His son Porter, the first boy born in Berlin, built the house at Scott Hill and Airport Road in 1815 (still standing). A descendant Henry Perrin still lived in 'Perrin territory' along Scott Hill Road into the modern era.

sources.label: Vermont Genealogy — Biography of Rev. James Hobart

residence.note: Settled May 1789 from Hebron CT; farmed 1300 acres at Scott Hill/East Road intersection in East Road School District.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Established the 1300-acre East Road farmstead and built permanent buildings here after settling Berlin VT in 1789.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT.

narrative_blocks.header: Settled Berlin Vermont with collapsed-roof cabin
narrative_blocks.body: Zachariah served in the Revolutionary War with the 8th company of the 12th Connecticut militia, then in February-March 1789 led his family from Hebron Connecticut to Berlin Vermont — up the Connecticut River with two teams of oxen, switching from wheels to runners in Brookfield. They found the log cabin he had built the previous summer crushed under three or four feet of snow.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded a 1300-acre farm still in family
narrative_blocks.body: Zachariah's 1300 acres in the East Road School District of Berlin VT — at what became the southeast corner of East Road and Scott Hill Road — passed down through generations of Perrins. His son Porter, the first boy born in Berlin, built the house at Scott Hill and Airport Road in 1815 (still standing). A descendant Henry Perrin still lived in 'Perrin territory' along Scott Hill Road into the modern era.

cross_connections.display_label: Settled Vermont 1789 after cousin David Newton's 1777 migration

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Lived on the Perrin family farmstead from her arrival in Berlin in 1789 until her death in 1828.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT — alongside Zachariah.

narrative_blocks.header: Walked to Vermont with two oxen teams
narrative_blocks.body: In February or March 1789 Mary and Zachariah moved their family from Hebron Connecticut to Berlin Vermont — traveling up the Connecticut River with two teams of oxen, then up the White River, switching from wheels to runners at Brookfield, then over a blazed trail. They arrived to find their log cabin's roof had collapsed under three to four feet of snow.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost firstborn daughter Mary in 1798
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's eldest child — also named Mary, born in Hebron — died in 1798. Four months later another daughter was born and named Mary in her sister's memory. The second Mary later married Julius Yemans Dewey and was the mother of Admiral George Dewey, hero of Manila Bay 1898.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT — FindAGrave 90950772.
Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT — alongside Betsey.

sources.label: Vermont Genealogy — Biography of Rev. James Hobart

narrative_blocks.header: Berlin Vermont's first settled minister thirty-one years
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained November 7, 1798 as Berlin Congregational's first settled minister, James drafted the church's confession of faith, covenant, and articles of discipline himself. His 31-year pastorate ran to May 1829, when a mutual council dismissed him. He then spent twelve years preaching in New Hampshire and twenty more itinerating around Worcester, Berlin, and West Berlin Vermont.

narrative_blocks.header: Walked six miles and preached at ninety-five
narrative_blocks.body: Per his obituary, 'in the very last year of his life, his 96th, he was able to preach a pretty well connected discourse, and could walk 6 or 8 miles in a day.' Below ordinary height with erect carriage, great memory, and a strong distinct voice, James was described as 'self-denying, laborious and persevering, having quite a missionary spirit.'

narrative_blocks.header: Three daughters married Congregational ministers
narrative_blocks.body: Three of James and Betsey's daughters married Congregational ministers across New England: Pamelia married Rev. Rufus Child (Gilmanton NH then Berlin), Julia married Rev. Pliny Fisk Barnard (Richmond ME then Williamstown VT then Westminster VT), and Hannah married Rev. George Craven (Methodist, Danville VT). The Berlin pulpit's reach extended through the next generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Walked six miles a day preaching at ninety-six
narrative_blocks.body: In the ninety-sixth year of his life Rev. Hobart preached well-connected discourses and was able to walk six or eight miles in a day. Below average height, he stood erect and was blessed with a retentive memory, strong voice, and good delivery.

education.notes: Apprentice studies with Asa Burton of Thetford VT before preaching license 1795.

career.notes: Settlement call from Nottingham.
Ordained November 7, 1798. Drew up church confession of faith, covenant, and articles of discipline. 31-year pastorate dismissed by mutual council May 1829.
Plymouth, Wentworth, Enfield, Alexandria, Bridgewater NH and near Portsmouth (12 years); then Worcester, Berlin, West Berlin VT (last 20 years).

burial.plot_notes: Worcester Village Cemetery, Worcester VT.
Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT — alongside Dr. Ira.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Civil War Medal of Honor recipient
narrative_blocks.body: Emeline Hobart Evans was mother of Ira Hobart Evans — Civil War Medal of Honor recipient and later Speaker of the Texas House — and of three other surviving children. Of her seven total children, only three reached middle age: Mary Emeline who married Captain Thomas Blossom Yale, Edward P. (1842-1913 Civil War 10th Vermont Infantry severely wounded Cold Harbor), and Ira Hobart Evans (1844-1922). The two Civil War sons gave the family a doubled Vermont-regiment combat record from the same Berlin Vermont household.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost four of seven children before adulthood
narrative_blocks.body: Emeline and Dr. Ira Evans's seven children included four childhood deaths: Mary E. (1834-1837, age 3); Ira (1836-1838, age 2 — the first attempt at the Ira name that her later son would carry); J. Annette (1840-1842, age 2); and William Dwight (1849-1861, age 12). All four were buried at Berlin Corner Cemetery in Vermont alongside their parents — a sequential-grief family burial cluster.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed 1852, moved family to Barre Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: After her husband Dr. Ira Evans died in 1852 at age forty-nine in Piermont New Hampshire, Emeline moved the family to Barre Vermont so her surviving sons could complete their education at Barre Academy — the pivot positioned both Edward and Ira Hobart Evans for their Civil War service paths. Emeline lived another forty-two years as widow, dying in 1894 around age eighty-three. She was born in 1811 in Berlin Vermont.

bio.bio_blurb: Minister’s wife who raised seven on the frontier

burial.plot_notes: Westminster New Cemetery, Westminster VT — alongside Pliny.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of seven during itinerant pastorate
narrative_blocks.body: Julia raised her seven children across her husband Pliny's wandering pastorates — Richmond Maine, Williamstown Vermont, Waitsfield Vermont, Westhampton Massachusetts, Westminster Vermont, Wendell Massachusetts, Dummerston Vermont. Their daughter Alice Theresa died of diphtheria at fifteen in 1866; the others reached adulthood and scattered across Vermont, New Hampshire, and Michigan.

narrative_blocks.header: Married one week, then off to the frontier
narrative_blocks.body: She wed her minister husband and a week later set out with him for his pastorate in the Maine backcountry. They raised seven children there and across Vermont — and buried one daughter, dead of diphtheria at fifteen.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT.

narrative_blocks.header: Stayed home in Berlin while siblings scattered
narrative_blocks.body: David was the only Hobart son to remain in Berlin Vermont through his life — three of his sisters' marriages took them to New Hampshire, Maine, Iowa, and Michigan, and his brothers James and the deceased Timothy Dwight scattered as well. David's eldest son Timothy Dwight Hobart, named for the Yale president and Hooker descendant, became a pioneering rancher and the founder of Pampa Texas.

naming_inspiration.description: Carries forward the name of Yale's Timothy Dwight, the Yale president and Hooker descendant remembered as the foremost American clergyman of his era.

career.notes: Elected Berlin VT's first town clerk at the inaugural town meeting March 31, 1791, alongside Zachariah Perrin (selectman) — his future father-in-law.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Built the family farmhouse in 1815 at Scott Hill Road and Airport Road.
Born and raised on the Perrin family farmstead at East Road and Scott Hill Road.

narrative_blocks.header: First male child born in Berlin Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: Porter was born February 1, 1790, less than a year after his parents Zachariah Perrin and Mary Talcott Perrin migrated by ox-sled from Hebron, Connecticut, to settle the eastern frontier of Berlin. He married Lucy Kinney of Plainfield in November 1815, lived his entire life in Berlin, and died there in May 1871 at age eighty-one.

narrative_blocks.header: Distributed his fortune to charity and his children
narrative_blocks.body: Porter accumulated more property in farming than any other man before him in Berlin, according to the 1881 Vermont Historical Gazetteer. He gave a large share to charitable and religious purposes during his lifetime, distributed much of the rest among his many children, and left enough for his widow's support to her death.

parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Jonathan Kinney of Plainfield VT. Not built — does not auto-qualify under Jalapeño Rule (no national notability). Berlin VT history (Child 1888) names him as Lucy's father.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners, Vermont.

career.notes: Served as Berlin VT superintendent of schools for over 20 years.
Represented Berlin in the Vermont state legislature on two occasions; in office during fall 1874 (October-November) per his son Porter Elisha's diary.
Continued the Perrin dairy operation on the family farm at Scott Hill Road and Airport Road; kept 36 head of cattle in 1874.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Talcott-line cousins served as elected lawmakers

narrative_blocks.header: Served Berlin schools twenty years and legislature twice
narrative_blocks.body: Justus Newton Perrin served as Berlin Vermont's superintendent of schools for more than twenty years and represented the town twice in the Vermont state legislature. He balanced public service with running the family dairy farm, where his fifteen-year-old son Porter Elisha documented the operation in an 1874 diary now held by the Vermont Historical Society.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived two wives and reached ninety-one
narrative_blocks.body: Justus's first wife Arethusa Hosford died in 1855 at age thirty, leaving him with two young daughters. He remarried in 1858 to Mary Wild and had five more children. He lived to ninety-one and is buried at Berlin Corner Cemetery alongside generations of his Perrin and Talcott ancestors.

education.notes: Albany Law School, Albany, New York.

career.notes: Prominent lawyer (per 1881 Berlin VT history) practicing in Indiana state and United States courts.

narrative_blocks.header: Vermont farm boy turned Indianapolis lawyer
narrative_blocks.body: Born on Porter Perrin's Berlin Vermont farm in 1827, George graduated from Brown University and Albany Law School and by 1881 had built a prominent practice in Indianapolis appearing in Indiana state and federal courts.

education.notes: Albany Law School, Albany, New York.

career.notes: Lawyer in Saint Johns MI in partnership with his younger brother MAJ Porter Kendrick Perrin (TD0184).

narrative_blocks.header: Michigan judge of probate and state senator
narrative_blocks.body: Henry left the Berlin Vermont farm for Dartmouth and Albany Law School, then settled in Saint Johns Michigan to practice law alongside his younger brother Major Porter Kendrick Perrin. He served as Clinton County's judge of probate and as a Michigan state senator.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Rest Cemetery, Saint Johns, Clinton County, Michigan. FindAGrave memorial.

education.notes: Albany Law School, Albany, New York.

career.notes: Lawyer in Saint Johns MI in partnership with his older brother Henry M. Perrin (TD0209).

military_service.unit: 27th Michigan Infantry Co. I → 2nd Michigan Infantry Staff
military_service.notes: Major commanding Company I, 27th Michigan Infantry; transferred to staff of 2nd Michigan Infantry. Specific battles not yet documented.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousin Ira Hobart Evans won Civil War Medal of Honor

narrative_blocks.header: Brother lawyers and Clinton County judges in Michigan
narrative_blocks.body: After the Civil War, Major Porter Kendrick joined his older brother Henry in Saint Johns Michigan to practice law. Both Perrin brothers served as Clinton County judges of probate; Henry also served as a Michigan state senator. Their niece Harriet Eliza later joined them in Saint Johns.

education.notes: Dartmouth College — interrupted by Civil War service, completed afterward.
Albany Law School, Albany, New York.

career.notes: Lawyer in Nashua, Chickasaw County, Iowa, after a brief residence in Burlington, Iowa. By 1881 doing a successful law business per Berlin VT history.

military_service.unit: Gov. Sprague's Cavalry (3 months) → 3rd Vermont Light Battery (~2 years)
military_service.notes: Initially enlisted from Dartmouth in Gov. Sprague's Cavalry for three months. Posted to Harper's Ferry; escaped with his company on the night before the Confederate capture of Harper's Ferry (September 14-15, 1862). Subsequently served approximately two years in the 3rd Vermont Light Battery. Mustered out as 1st Lieutenant. Returned to Dartmouth to complete his degree.

cross_connections.display_label: Both fought for Union as Talcott-line cousins

narrative_blocks.header: Escaped Harper's Ferry the night before surrender
narrative_blocks.body: William left Dartmouth to enlist in Gov. Sprague's Cavalry for three months and found himself posted to Harper's Ferry just before its September 1862 capture. His company escaped under cover of darkness the night before the surrender, then he transferred to the 3rd Vermont Light Battery for two more years and mustered out as a First Lieutenant.

death.cause: died at age thirty-one. Berlin VT history (Child 1888) attributes death to alcoholism that began as a medical prescription: his attending physician prescribed brandy as treatment for the ill health he developed in South Carolina, and the resulting addiction killed him. Talcott Pedigree (1876) silent on cause. Source-discrepancy preserved as historical record.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave memorial 103993.

education.notes: Class of 1812 per Talcott Pedigree (1876); Berlin VT history (Child 1888) gives 1813. Discrepancy unresolved.

career.notes: Preached in various places in Vermont, New York, and Pennsylvania.
Three-year pastorate in South Carolina taken for health reasons; did not recover; returned to Berlin.

narrative_blocks.header: Pastor in South Carolina sent home to die
narrative_blocks.body: Poor health led Rev. Perrin to accept a three-year pastorate in South Carolina, hoping a warmer climate would restore him. He did not recover and returned to Berlin Vermont, where he died in February 1824 at age thirty-one.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote two volumes of poetry before age thirty-one
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Perrin published two volumes of poetry as a Congregational minister preaching in Vermont, New York, and Pennsylvania. A third volume titled Eloida is rumored but no extant copy has been found.

narrative_blocks.header: Brandy prescribed as medicine became fatal addiction
narrative_blocks.body: Per the 1888 Berlin Vermont history, Rev. Perrin's attending physician prescribed brandy as a medical treatment for the ill health that drove him from his South Carolina pastorate back to Berlin. The prescription created an addiction that the source frankly attributes as the cause of his death at thirty-one.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Daniel Thompson of Montpelier VT. Not built — does not auto-qualify under Jalapeño Rule (no national notability). Berlin VT history (Child 1888) names him as Fanny's father.

burial.plot_notes: East Road Cemetery, Berlin VT — solo Hooker-tree connection, no CEM entry built per §0.4 threshold. Note: Cemetery sits adjacent to historic Perrin family land at East Road / Scott Hill intersection in Berlin VT.

narrative_blocks.header: Berlin Vermont son who took the gospel south
narrative_blocks.body: Truman was born in Berlin Vermont in 1796 to the Perrin family's newly-cleared 1300-acre Scott Hill farm. He took the Presbyterian ministry as his calling and migrated south through Alabama and Mississippi where his three children grew up — but he died in Massachusetts in 1869, and was returned to Vermont for burial near the family land where he had been born.

narrative_blocks.header: Southern bank failures ruined him by 1850
narrative_blocks.body: After years preaching and accumulating merchant property in Alabama and Georgia, the failure of several Southern banks around 1850 wiped out most of his fortune. He returned to Vermont and spent his remaining nineteen years 'preaching in various places, and in the employ of the American Tract Society.'

narrative_blocks.header: Wife stayed in Aberdeen Mississippi when he returned
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Pronecy Tindall remained in Aberdeen Mississippi when he moved back north around 1850. He died at Washington Massachusetts in November 1869; she lived another fifteen years in Aberdeen, dying there in 1884.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried Vermont family land though died Massachusetts
narrative_blocks.body: Despite dying at Washington Berkshire County Massachusetts in November 1869, he was buried at East Road Cemetery in Berlin Vermont — adjacent to the 1300-acre Perrin family land his father Zachariah had established in 1789. His parents had chosen a different Berlin cemetery; Truman's plot lay closer to the farm.

career.notes: Per Pronecy's 1884 obituary 'the late Presbyterian minister.' Migration history: Berlin VT (b.1796) → Alabama (children born early 1830s) → Mississippi (raised William in Aberdeen) → Massachusetts (d.1869 Washington Berkshire County).

residence.note: Settled Aberdeen MS after estrangement from Truman; remained until 1884 death.

burial.plot_notes: Odd Fellows Rest Cemetery, Aberdeen MS — Perrin family plot, alongside daughter Mary Caroline Pollard.

parents.father_research_notes: Father Dr. John Lewis Tindall Sr. of Smithland Livingston County KY (d. between 1884 and the 1884 obit; her brother Dr. John Lewis Tindall Jr. d. May 15, 1884 just two months before Pronecy). Not built — non-tree.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother Mary Ritchey Tindall. Not built.

burial.plot_notes: Odd Fellows Rest Cemetery, Aberdeen MS — Perrin family plot, predeceased mother Pronecy by 18 years.

residence.note: Final residence with son in Midland TX where he died 3:00 PM Saturday July 25, 1903.

burial.plot_notes: Burial details unknown per FindAGrave.

marriages.officiant: Rev. Dr. Spencer (likely Capt. John Thomas Spencer of Floridian Academy, Greensburg LA)

narrative_blocks.header: LL.B. four months before the Civil War
narrative_blocks.body: William graduated with his Bachelor of Laws from the Law Department of the University of Louisiana — what is now Tulane University — on Saturday March 30, 1861. Four months later he married Clara Niven on the eve of his 28th birthday at Osyka Mississippi; less than a year after that he enlisted in the Confederate Washington Artillery Battalion at New Orleans.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his daughter's wedding host nine days later
narrative_blocks.body: After his first wife Clara died Waxahachie Texas in January 1886, William moved east to Thornton Calhoun County Arkansas. His daughter Louise married Dr. Daniel Frizzell at Camden Arkansas on October 8, 1889 — nine days later October 17, 1889 William himself married Mrs. Sarah Bullard at Thornton.

narrative_blocks.header: First cousin to Admiral Dewey and Speaker Evans
narrative_blocks.body: Through his grandmother Mary Talcott Perrin and grandfather Zachariah Perrin, William was first cousin to Admiral George Dewey of Manila Bay (whose mother Mary Polly was William's aunt) and a cousin twice removed of Civil War Medal of Honor recipient Ira Hobart Evans of Texas and of Pampa Texas's Timothy Dwight Hobart. The Vermont Perrin matriarch's grandchildren scattered across Confederate Mississippi, Union Vermont, Texas frontiers, and the Spanish-American War Pacific.

education.school_name: Law Department, University of Louisiana (now Tulane University)
education.notes: LL.B. (Bachelor of Laws) — graduated just before Civil War.

career.location: Greensburg, Amite, and New Orleans, Louisiana

career.organization: Smithville Male and Female Normal Institute
career.notes: Principal of the Institute; taught alongside wife Clara, Mrs. Clay Elliott, Professor William Saville, and Dr. John T. M. Gregory. NOT BUILT as INST per Sam directive.
Later in life.
1900 U.S. Census enumerator for Midland County TX.

military_service.unit: 2nd Company, Washington Artillery Battalion of Louisiana
military_service.notes: Enlisted Private March 7, 1862 New Orleans LA. Service record shows present on all rolls through October 1863; absent with leave late 1863; absent without leave early 1864; returned active duty March 1864; served until February 1865. Occupation recorded as 'lawyer'; residence Osyka MS. Confederate service during the Civil War.

burial.plot_notes: Green Mount Cemetery, Montpelier VT — alongside Julius.

narrative_blocks.header: Burned the log cabin where she was born
narrative_blocks.body: Per family lore, Mary Polly was 'ashamed of having been born in a log cabin' — she and a sister later went up one night and burned the original Perrin cabin to the ground. Her father Zachariah's permanent farm at Scott Hill in Berlin Vermont stood on the same land, but the dirt-floor structure of her birth was gone by her own hand.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Manila Bay's Admiral, died at forty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Polly bore Admiral George Dewey on December 26, 1837 — her third child. He was five years old when she died at age 44 in September 1843 Montpelier Vermont. Her husband Julius later married twice more, but Mary Polly was the mother of all his children.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her elder sister Mary Perrin (1781-1798), born Hebron CT, who died at age seventeen — four months before her parents named the second baby Mary in her memory. The second Mary was sometimes called Polly to distinguish from her dead namesake.

burial.plot_notes: Green Mount Cemetery, Montpelier VT — Latitude 44.257843, Longitude -72.5944519.

parents.father_research_notes: Father Capt. Simeon Dewey (1770-1863), born Hebron CT, settled Berlin VT four miles from Montpelier, lived to age 93. m. Prudence Yemans (1772-1844) on February 27, 1794. Not built — non-tree.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother Prudence Yemans Dewey (1772-1844). Not built — non-tree.

sources.label: Vermont Historical Society — Dr. Julius Yemans Dewey portrait

notable.notable_blurb: Vermont physician; founded National Life Insurance

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Vermont's largest life insurance company
narrative_blocks.body: Julius founded the National Life Insurance Company at Montpelier in 1850 and served as its president until his death twenty-seven years later. He personally delivered the remittance for the company's first claim, prompting a public thank-you from the surviving family. NLI remains one of the ten oldest insurance companies in the United States.

narrative_blocks.header: Married three Susans and one Mary Perrin
narrative_blocks.body: After his Berlin sweetheart Mary Polly Perrin died in 1843 leaving four young children, Julius married twice more — both wives named Susan, neither bore him children. His third wife Susan Elizabeth Griggs Lilley had a daughter from a prior marriage who later married Julius's own son Edward — making Edward and his stepsister-in-law husband and wife.

narrative_blocks.header: Backed his son's beating from Pangborn the teacher
narrative_blocks.body: When eleven-year-old George Dewey led an attack on his Montpelier schoolteacher Z.K. Pangborn — and Pangborn flogged the boys into submission with a stick of stove fuel and walked the bruised George home to explain — Julius listened gravely, then thanked the teacher and asked him to repeat the punishment whenever necessary. George later credited the discipline with saving him from prison.

education.school_name: American Literary Scientific and Military Academy (Norwich Military School)
education.notes: Norwich Military School (later Norwich University) attended pre-medical.

career.notes: Practiced as 'most prominent practitioner and one of the leading citizens of the capital of Vermont' until founding NLI.
Founded 1850 (some sources 1848). Personally delivered the company's first claim remittance, prompting public thank-you from surviving family. Served as president until his death.
Documented founder of Christ Episcopal of Montpelier.

institutions.institution_blurb: Founder and president of the National Life Insurance Company of Montpelier — Vermont's largest life insurer.
Trustee of Norwich University; previously attended its predecessor American Literary Scientific and Military Academy.

artworks.blurb: Vermont Historical Society oil portrait c.1860, gift of Susan Howland estate.

burial.plot_notes: Green Mount Cemetery, Montpelier VT.

career.notes: Succeeded his father Julius as NLI president 1877. Eldest of the four Dewey siblings; remained based in Montpelier VT.

institutions.institution_blurb: Succeeded his father Julius as president of the National Life Insurance Company.

residence.note: 120 State Street; built new home 1889 on family site, moved old house to 144 State Street.

burial.plot_notes: Green Mount Cemetery, Montpelier VT.

sources.label: Vermont Historical Society — Edward Dewey 8th Vermont Papers (1864)
sources.note: Quartermaster invoices and inventories from one year of regiment service.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his stepsister-in-law in 1856
narrative_blocks.body: In August 1856 Edward married Susan Griggs Lilley — the daughter of his father Julius's third wife Susan Elizabeth Griggs Lilley (married 1855). Edward and Susan had been stepsiblings-by-marriage for one year before becoming husband and wife. They had six children, four documented in this tree.

narrative_blocks.header: Quartermaster at Cedar Creek and Opequon
narrative_blocks.body: Edward joined the 8th Vermont Volunteer Infantry as Lieutenant Quartermaster in Louisiana January 1864, accompanied the regiment to Virginia in July, and saw action at Opequon and Cedar Creek. He was promoted Captain in February 1865, resigned three months later, and returned home. His quartermaster invoices for 1864 survive at the Vermont Historical Society.

narrative_blocks.header: Built the 1889 Queen Anne State Street mansion
narrative_blocks.body: Edward commissioned a Queen Anne brick house at 120 State Street Montpelier in 1889 — designed by city mayor and architect George Guernsey on the site of his family's earlier home. The old house was moved to 144 State Street where it stood until 1969. Edward's 1889 house was moved to 128 State Street in 1948 to make way for the Vermont State Office Building.

career.notes: Prepared for college but chose mercantile life until 1860.
Four-year tenure before Civil War service.
Returned from Civil War and joined NLI under his father's presidency. Elected vice-president June 1877; held position to January 1897.

military_service.notes: Joined 8th VT Volunteers as Quartermaster in Louisiana January 1864; accompanied regiment to Virginia July 1864; participated in battles of Opequon and Cedar Creek. Promoted Captain and Assistant Quartermaster, U.S. volunteers staff department February 11, 1865; resigned three months later. Quartermaster papers preserved at Vermont Historical Society.

institutions.institution_blurb: Civil War quartermaster who returned to serve as assistant secretary then vice-president of his father Julius's National Life Insurance Company until 1897.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Built the 1889 Queen Anne brick house at 120 State Street Montpelier (designed by mayor George Guernsey) on the site of his family home.

burial.plot_notes: Bethlehem Chapel crypt level, Washington National Cathedral, Washington DC. Originally interred Arlington National Cemetery 1917; remains transferred 1925 by his widow Mildred.

notable.notable_blurb: Admiral of the Navy; Manila Bay 1898 hero

narrative_blocks.header: Mother dead at five; Norwich expelled him
narrative_blocks.body: George was five when his mother Mary Polly Perrin Dewey died in 1843. His father remarried twice and George was raised under stern discipline — at eleven, after George led a schoolyard rebellion, his father backed the teacher's beating of him with a stick of stove fuel. At fifteen George went to Norwich Military School; at seventeen Norwich expelled him for drunkenness and herding sheep into the barracks.

narrative_blocks.header: Steered the Mississippi past Forts Jackson and Philip
narrative_blocks.body: As Executive Lieutenant of USS Mississippi at the start of the Civil War, George was at the helm when David Farragut's first division ran past the Confederate defenses on April 24-25, 1862. He maneuvered Mississippi through shallows, evaded a ramming attack by the ironclad CSS Manassas, then ran the ironclad down — Manassas ran aground, was abandoned, and a boat from Mississippi set her on fire.

narrative_blocks.header: Six hours sank the Spanish Pacific fleet
narrative_blocks.body: 'You may fire when you are ready, Gridley' — Dewey opened the Battle of Manila Bay at first light May 1, 1898, and in six hours his Asiatic Squadron sank or captured the entire Spanish Pacific fleet under Adm. Patricio Montojo y Pasarón, with one American casualty (heart attack). He was promoted rear admiral that May, full admiral the next year, and Admiral of the Navy by Act of Congress 1903 — the only person ever to hold the rank.

narrative_blocks.header: Wore his Manila Bay medal reversed for modesty
narrative_blocks.body: The Battle of Manila Bay Medal — designed by Daniel Chester French and produced by Tiffany & Co. — bore Dewey's profile on the obverse, so he wore his medal reversed out of modesty. He was one of only four Americans (with Sampson, Byrd, and Pershing) ever entitled to wear a US government medal carrying their own image.

narrative_blocks.header: Briefly Democratic presidential candidate, then withdrew
narrative_blocks.body: In 1900 Dewey explored the Democratic presidential nomination and made a string of public missteps — calling the presidency easy, admitting he had never voted in a presidential election, and prophetically predicting 'Our next war will be with Germany.' Critics also attacked his Catholic marriage to Mildred Hazen and his transferring his $50,000 public-gift mansion to her. He withdrew in mid-May and endorsed William McKinley.

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, a governor of New Hampshire

education.notes: American Literary Scientific and Military Academy / Norwich Military School / Norwich University. Two years 1852-1854. Per Wikipedia expelled 1854 for drunkenness and herding sheep into the barracks.
Entered United States Naval Academy at age 16 (September 23, 1854). Of all who entered, only fourteen completed the course. Graduated June 18, 1858 fifth in class.

career.notes: Helmed Mississippi past Forts Jackson and St. Philip April 24-25, 1862. Battle of Port Hudson March 14, 1863 — Mississippi ran aground, abandoned; Dewey among last to leave.
Recommended for promotion after Donaldsonville LA action 1863.
Second Battle of Fort Fisher January 13-15, 1865 — promoted Lieutenant Commander after action New York Times called 'the most beautiful duel of the war.'

career.organization: USS Constitution and other vessels at US Naval Academy
career.notes: Including command of USS Constitution as training ship.
Promoted Commander.
Promoted Captain 1884. Hospitalized Malta 1882-1884 with liver abscess and typhoid.
Flagship duty.
Position secured by Theodore Roosevelt over more senior officers. Began war preparations as US-Spain tensions accelerated.

BATTLE OF MANILA BAY May 1, 1898: 'You may fire when you are ready, Gridley.' Sank or captured entire Spanish Pacific fleet under Admiral Patricio Montojo y Pasarón in 6 hours; only one American casualty (heart attack). August 13, 1898 capture of Manila with Gen. Wesley Merritt.

career.organization: General Board of the United States Navy
career.notes: Newly established Navy Department major policy-making body. Championed naval aviation and submarine development. Served until death.

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended Norwich Military School age 15-17; expelled 1854 per Wikipedia. Dewey Hall built 1899 in his honor.
Entered Naval Academy 1854; graduated fifth of fourteen 1858; instructor 1868-1870; laid Memorial Chapel cornerstone June 1904.
Honorary member of the New York Society of the Cincinnati 1901.

quotes.text: You may fire when you are ready, Gridley.
quotes.attribution: Adm. George Dewey to Capt. Charles V. Gridley, USS Olympia, opening Battle of Manila Bay, 1 May 1898

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name carries her maternal grandmother Mary Talcott Perrin's married surname forward — preserving the Berlin Vermont Perrin line in the Dewey generation.

burialCemetery.name: Berlin Corner Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners Vermont. Burial site of the Perrin-Hobart founding family of Berlin VT — Zachariah Perrin Esq. (X01832, Berlin VT 1789 founder, Rev War vet, d.1838 age 90), his wife Mary Talcott Perrin (T01387, d.1828), their daughter Betsey Perrin Hobart (TD0120, d.1876), her husband Rev. James Hobart (X02368, Berlin VT first settled minister, d.1862 age 95), their son David Hobart (TD0129, d.1901), and his wife Caroline Reed Hobart (X02370, d.1898). Three Perrin-Hobart generations. [pilot100] Backfilled with Evans/Yale Berlin VT generation: Dr. Ira Evans Sr. (X02383) and Emeline Hobart Evans (TD0124, daughter of Rev. James + Betsey Hobart); their seven children including 4 died young/childhood/infancy (Mary E., Ira Jr., J. Annette, William Dwight) and 3 adult-lifespan (Mary Emeline Evans Yale + her Civil War vet husband Capt. Thomas Blossom Yale 108th NY Inf, Edward P. Evans 10th VT Inf wounded Cold Harbor, and Civil War Medal of Honor recipient Ira Hobart Evans TD0163 who died San Diego CA 1922 and was returned to Berlin Corner per his wishes). Cemetery now spans four generations of Hobart-Evans-Yale family across ~150 years.
